enrichment.short_description: Segment (Twilio Segment) is a customer data platform that collects, unifies, and routes first-party customer data from websites, apps, and backend systems to hundreds of downstream marketing, analytics, and data warehouse destinations.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, usage-based on Monthly Tracked Users (MTUs) with custom enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 1,000 MTUs per month; Team plan starts at $120 per month for up to 10,000 MTUs
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 500 employees within the Segment business unit; part of Twilio Inc., which has roughly 5,500+ employees company-wide
enrichment.seo_title: Segment (Twilio Segment) Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Segment, now Twilio Segment, is a customer data platform (CDP). See 2026 pricing (Free, Team, Business), key features, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Segment is a customer data platform (CDP) originally founded in 2011 as Segment.io in San Francisco, California, that helps companies collect first-party customer event data from websites, mobile apps, and backend systems, unify it into a single customer profile, and route it in real time to hundreds of downstream tools such as analytics platforms, advertising networks, CRMs, and data warehouses. Twilio, the cloud communications company, acquired Segment in November 2020 in an all-stock deal valued at approximately 3.2 billion dollars, and the product has since operated as Twilio Segment, retaining its original branding while being integrated into Twilio's broader customer engagement platform alongside products like Twilio Engage. Segment's core product, called Connections, offers software development kits and a large catalog of pre-built integrations that let engineering and marketing teams instrument tracking once and send that data everywhere without writing custom code for each destination, plus governance tooling called Protocols that enforces tracking-plan data quality and a Privacy Portal for handling data subject requests under regulations like GDPR and CCPA. Higher tiers add Unify, an identity resolution engine that merges anonymous and known user activity into a single customer profile, and Reverse ETL, which syncs warehouse data back into operational tools. Pricing is based on Monthly Tracked Users (MTUs), meaning the number of unique users or devices tracked across all of a company's connected sources in a given month. The Free plan supports up to 1,000 MTUs with a limited number of destinations. The Team plan, Segment's entry-level paid tier, starts at 120 dollars per month for up to 10,000 MTUs and unlocks unlimited destinations plus basic governance features like Protocols and the Privacy Portal. The Business plan is not publicly priced and requires a custom quote from Segment's sales team; real-world contract values for the Business tier commonly range from roughly 25,000 to over 500,000 dollars per year depending on MTU volume, with mid-market deals typically in the 25,000 to 100,000 dollar range and larger enterprise deals reaching well into six figures. Business tier unlocks the full CDP feature set, including Unify identity resolution, advanced Protocols, Reverse ETL, Twilio Engage for building marketing audiences and journeys, CustomerAI predictive features, and dedicated enterprise support with an SLA. Segment competes with customer data platforms like mParticle, RudderStack, and Amplitude, and remains one of the most widely adopted CDPs among mid-market and enterprise SaaS, e-commerce, and media companies for centralizing customer event data across a fragmented martech stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Segment was founded in 2011 in San Francisco as a customer data infrastructure company, building tools that let engineering teams collect event data once and send it to many downstream analytics and marketing tools without custom integration work for each one. In November 2020, Twilio acquired Segment for approximately 3.2 billion dollars in an all-stock transaction, and the product now operates as Twilio Segment.

Segment's core offering is a customer data platform (CDP) that unifies data from web, mobile, and server-side sources into a single customer profile and routes it to hundreds of destinations, including analytics tools, advertising platforms, CRMs, and data warehouses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Segment's Connections product provides SDKs and a large catalog of pre-built destination integrations, so teams instrument tracking once and distribute data broadly. Protocols enforces data quality against a defined tracking plan, and the Privacy Portal helps teams manage data subject access and deletion requests for privacy regulations.

Higher tiers add Unify, an identity resolution engine that merges anonymous and known user activity into one profile, and Reverse ETL, which syncs data from a warehouse back into operational and marketing tools. The Business tier also includes Twilio Engage for building audiences and multi-channel customer journeys.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Segment prices primarily by Monthly Tracked Users (MTUs). The Free plan covers up to 1,000 MTUs with limited destinations. The Team plan starts at 120 dollars per month for up to 10,000 MTUs, unlimited destinations, and basic governance tools, with cost scaling as MTU volume grows.

The Business plan is custom-quoted based on MTU volume and required features, with typical real-world contracts ranging from tens of thousands to several hundred thousand dollars per year for larger enterprise deployments.

features.title: Cross-platform data collection
features.description: SDKs for web, mobile, and server-side sources let teams instrument customer event tracking once across their entire stack.

features.title: Hundreds of pre-built destinations
features.description: A large catalog of integrations routes collected data to analytics, advertising, CRM, and data warehouse tools without custom coding.

features.title: Protocols data governance
features.description: Enforces a defined tracking plan to catch and prevent bad or inconsistent event data before it reaches downstream tools.

features.title: Unify identity resolution
features.description: Merges anonymous and known user activity into a single, deduplicated customer profile across devices and sessions.

features.title: Reverse ETL
features.description: Syncs data from a cloud data warehouse back into operational tools like CRMs and support platforms.

features.title: Privacy Portal
features.description: Helps teams manage GDPR and CCPA data subject access and deletion requests across connected tools.

features.title: Twilio Engage
features.description: Builds customer audiences and orchestrates multi-channel marketing journeys using unified Segment data, available on Business tier.

features.title: Free tier for smaller projects
features.description: A free plan supports up to 1,000 monthly tracked users, letting small teams and side projects use Segment at no cost.

pros: Very large catalog of pre-built destination integrations reduces custom engineering work
Free tier makes it accessible for small projects and early-stage startups
Identity resolution (Unify) provides a genuinely unified customer profile across devices
Backed by Twilio's infrastructure and enterprise support resources
Strong data governance tooling (Protocols, Privacy Portal) for regulated industries
cons: Business tier pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation, making budgeting harder
MTU-based pricing can scale quickly and unpredictably as a company's user base grows
Free and Team tiers lack advanced governance and identity resolution features
Enterprise contracts can run into six figures annually for larger MTU volumes
Some advanced AI and audience features are reserved for the highest tier only

faqs.question: Is Segment free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Segment offers a free plan that supports up to 1,000 monthly tracked users (MTUs) with a limited number of destinations.

faqs.question: How much does Segment's Team plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Team plan starts at 120 dollars per month for up to 10,000 MTUs, with cost scaling as MTU volume increases.

faqs.question: Who owns Segment now?
faqs.answer: Segment is owned by Twilio, which acquired the company in November 2020 for approximately 3.2 billion dollars in an all-stock deal. It operates as Twilio Segment.

faqs.question: What does MTU mean in Segment's pricing?
faqs.answer: MTU stands for Monthly Tracked User, referring to the number of unique users or devices tracked across all of a company's connected sources in a given month.

faqs.question: When was Segment founded?
faqs.answer: Segment was founded in 2011 in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: What is Segment Unify?
faqs.answer: Unify is Segment's identity resolution feature that merges anonymous and known user activity into a single customer profile, available on the Business tier.

faqs.question: Does Segment offer Reverse ETL?
faqs.answer: Yes, Segment's Reverse ETL feature syncs data from a cloud data warehouse back into operational and marketing tools.

faqs.question: What are Segment's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Segment competes with other customer data platforms such as mParticle, RudderStack, and Amplitude.

use_cases.title: Unified analytics instrumentation
use_cases.description: An engineering team uses Segment to instrument event tracking once across web and mobile apps and route that data to multiple analytics tools without writing separate integrations.

use_cases.title: Marketing audience building
use_cases.description: A marketing team uses Segment's Unify and Twilio Engage features to build unified customer profiles and launch targeted multi-channel campaigns.

use_cases.title: Data warehouse synchronization
use_cases.description: A data team uses Segment's Reverse ETL to sync curated customer data from their warehouse back into CRM and support tools used by frontline teams.

features: Access to the Segment Connections catalog
Up to 10,000 MTUs included, additional usage billed as volume grows

enrichment.short_description: Sejda PDF is a browser-based and desktop PDF editor that lets users edit text and images, merge and split files, fill and sign forms, run OCR, and convert PDFs, with a limited free tier and paid plans for heavier use.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription (free tier with daily task limits, paid weekly, monthly, and annual plans)
enrichment.starting_price: Free with daily task limits; paid plans start around EUR 5.50 for a 7-day Web Week Pass
enrichment.company_size: Small company, exact headcount not publicly disclosed (estimated under 50 employees)
enrichment.seo_title: Sejda PDF Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sejda PDF is a browser and desktop PDF editor. See 2026 pricing plans, free tier limits, key features like OCR and e-signatures, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sejda PDF is a PDF editing tool that started in 2010 as an online project and is now operated by Sejda B.V., a company headquartered in Amsterdam, the Netherlands. It offers two main products: Sejda Web, a browser-based tool usable on any operating system without installation, and Sejda Desktop, a downloadable application for Windows, macOS, and Linux. The platform provides more than 30 individual PDF tools covering editing text and images directly inside a PDF, adding annotations and watermarks, merging and splitting files, rotating and reordering pages, converting to and from PDF (including Word, Excel, and image formats), filling and generating forms, applying electronic signatures, running optical character recognition (OCR) to make scanned documents searchable, setting access permissions and passwords, and batch-processing multiple files at once; some plans also expose API access for developers who want to integrate Sejda's PDF processing into their own applications. The free plan is usable without an account but is capped at a small number of tasks per day (commonly cited as around three), smaller file size limits, and restricted access to certain premium tools, which pushes frequent users toward a paid plan. Paid pricing is denominated in euros and structured around three main plans: a non-recurring Web Week Pass priced at 5.50 euros per user for seven days of full Sejda Web access, a recurring Web Monthly plan at 8.50 euros per user per month for ongoing browser-based access, and a Desktop+Web Annual plan at 71 euros per user per year that bundles a year of both the desktop application and web access at roughly a 30 percent discount versus paying monthly; a volume discount of 20 percent is available for organizations buying 10 or more user licenses. All paid tiers include unlimited documents, OCR support for up to 100 pages per task, batch processing, longer task-processing time allowances (up to about 21 minutes for large jobs), and larger file uploads up to 500MB, compared to the more restrictive free tier. Sejda targets a broad mix of freelancers, small businesses, and mid-size and large enterprises who need occasional to moderate PDF editing without adopting a heavier, more expensive suite like Adobe Acrobat, and it markets itself on simplicity, browser accessibility, and privacy, including automatic deletion of uploaded files from its servers after a short retention window. The tool draws significant organic traffic as a search-driven utility site, and it competes with other lightweight PDF editors such as PDFescape, Smallpdf, and iLovePDF in the crowded browser-based PDF tooling category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sejda PDF began as an online PDF editing project in 2010 and is operated today by Sejda B.V., a company based in Amsterdam, the Netherlands. It offers both a browser-based product, Sejda Web, that works without installation on any operating system, and a downloadable desktop application, Sejda Desktop, for Windows, macOS, and Linux.
The platform is built around simplicity and privacy, offering more than 30 discrete PDF tools while promoting automatic deletion of user-uploaded files from its servers shortly after processing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sejda covers the core PDF editing workflow: editing text and images inline, merging and splitting documents, reordering and rotating pages, converting to and from formats like Word and Excel, filling and creating forms, applying electronic signatures, and setting password protection and permissions.

Paid plans add OCR for up to 100 pages per task to make scanned documents searchable, batch processing of multiple files at once, longer processing time allowances for large jobs, and larger file upload limits, along with API access on certain plans for developers who want to automate PDF processing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sejda's free plan allows a limited number of tasks per day with smaller file size limits and no account required. Paid options are priced in euros: a non-recurring Web Week Pass at 5.50 euros per user for 7 days, a recurring Web Monthly plan at 8.50 euros per user per month, and a Desktop+Web Annual plan at 71 euros per user per year that bundles both products at roughly a 30 percent discount versus monthly billing.
A 20 percent volume discount applies for organizations purchasing 10 or more user licenses at once, making Sejda more cost-effective for small teams that need PDF editing across multiple seats.

features.title: Text and image editing
features.description: Edit existing text and images directly inside a PDF without needing to recreate the document from scratch.

features.title: Merge, split, and reorder pages
features.description: Combine multiple PDFs into one file, split a document into separate files, or rearrange and rotate individual pages.

features.title: Format conversion
features.description: Convert PDFs to and from formats like Word, Excel, and images, and generate PDFs from other file types.

features.title: OCR for scanned documents
features.description: Run optical character recognition on up to 100 pages per task on paid plans to make scanned PDFs searchable and editable.

features.title: Electronic signatures and forms
features.description: Fill out existing PDF forms, create new fillable forms, and apply electronic signatures to documents.

features.title: Batch processing
features.description: Process multiple PDF files at once for tasks like compressing, converting, or watermarking, available on paid plans.

features.title: Password protection and permissions
features.description: Set passwords and access permissions to restrict who can open, edit, or print a PDF.

features.title: Desktop and browser access
features.description: Use Sejda entirely in a web browser with no installation, or install the Sejda Desktop app for offline use on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

pros: Simple, focused interface that is easy to use without training
Available both as a browser tool and a desktop app for offline use
OCR, e-signatures, and batch processing are included on paid plans at a relatively low price point
Automatic deletion of uploaded files from servers supports privacy-conscious use
Volume discounts make it reasonably affordable for small teams
cons: Free plan is capped at a small number of daily tasks, pushing regular users toward a paid plan
Pricing is listed in euros, which can be less convenient for some non-European buyers
Lacks the deeper enterprise document workflow and compliance features of tools like Adobe Acrobat
OCR is capped at 100 pages per task even on paid plans
No dedicated mobile app; browser use on mobile devices can be less convenient than desktop

faqs.question: Is Sejda PDF free to use?
faqs.answer: Sejda offers a free plan with no account required, but it is limited to a small number of tasks per day and smaller file sizes. Paid plans remove these limits.

faqs.question: How much does Sejda PDF cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 5.50 euros per user for a 7-day Web Week Pass, 8.50 euros per user per month for the Web Monthly plan, or 71 euros per user per year for the Desktop+Web Annual plan.

faqs.question: Does Sejda offer a desktop app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sejda Desktop is a downloadable application available for Windows, macOS, and Linux, sold as part of the Desktop+Web Annual plan.

faqs.question: Can Sejda run OCR on scanned PDFs?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid Sejda plans include OCR support for up to 100 pages per task, making scanned documents searchable and editable.

faqs.question: Where is Sejda based?
faqs.answer: Sejda is operated by Sejda B.V., a company headquartered in Amsterdam, the Netherlands.

faqs.question: When was Sejda founded?
faqs.answer: Sejda began as an online PDF editing project in 2010.

faqs.question: Does Sejda offer a volume discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, a 20 percent discount applies for organizations purchasing 10 or more user licenses.

faqs.question: Does Sejda delete uploaded files?
faqs.answer: Sejda states that files uploaded for processing are automatically deleted from its servers a short time after processing, supporting user privacy.

use_cases.title: Occasional PDF editing for freelancers
use_cases.description: A freelancer uses Sejda's free or low-cost Web Week Pass plan to quickly edit, sign, or merge PDF documents for client work without installing software.

use_cases.title: Small business document workflows
use_cases.description: A small business team uses the Desktop+Web Annual plan to fill forms, apply signatures, and convert documents as part of regular administrative work.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and office staff

use_cases.title: Digitizing scanned paperwork
use_cases.description: An organization uses Sejda's OCR tool to convert scanned paper documents into searchable, editable PDFs for archiving or further processing.

features: Limited number of tasks per day (approximately 3)
Restricted access to some premium tools
7 days of full Sejda Web access
Ongoing browser-based access to all Sejda Web tools
OCR up to 100 pages per task
A year of Sejda Desktop access for Windows, macOS, and Linux
500MB file uploads and extended task processing time

enrichment.short_description: Selfoss is a free, open-source, self-hosted PHP web application for aggregating RSS feeds, social media, and other content sources into a single, mobile-friendly reading interface.
enrichment.company_size: Community-maintained open-source project, not a company
enrichment.seo_title: Selfoss Review 2026: Features, Setup and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Selfoss is a free, open-source, self-hosted RSS reader and content aggregator. See its features, setup requirements, pricing (it is free), and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Selfoss is a free, open-source, self-hosted RSS reader and multi-source content aggregator originally created by developer Tobias Zeising, with its first public commit dating to October 2011. Written in PHP, Selfoss is designed to be lightweight, with an installed footprint of roughly 25MB, and it can be deployed on virtually any web host or home server that supports PHP and one of its supported databases, which include MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite. Rather than being limited to RSS and Atom feeds alone, Selfoss functions as a general-purpose content mashup tool: through its open plugin system, developers and users can write custom data connectors to pull in content from social networks and other web sources, combining everything into a single unified reading timeline. The interface uses an adaptive, mobile-friendly layout that works on phones and tablets as well as desktop browsers, and feeds can be organized into categories and bulk-imported using standard OPML subscription files, making migration from other feed readers straightforward. Selfoss exposes a RESTful JSON API, which has enabled a small ecosystem of third-party and community-built mobile apps and browser extensions to interact with a self-hosted instance beyond its built-in web interface. Because feed content needs to be refreshed periodically, Selfoss relies on a scheduled job, either an HTTP-triggered endpoint or a command-line invocation run via cron or a systemd timer, to fetch new items from subscribed sources rather than polling continuously in the background. The project is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPL-3.0) or, at the user's option, any later version, which permits free personal and commercial use, including operating a hosted instance for others, as long as modifications and distributions remain open source under the same license terms. After its original creator stepped back from active involvement, the project has continued to be maintained by community contributors, notably Jan Tojnar, in their free time, which is typical of small self-hosted open-source utilities. Selfoss has no company behind it, no pricing tiers, and no official support contracts; it is popular within the self-hosting and home-lab community, frequently appearing on curated lists of self-hosted applications alongside tools like FreshRSS, Miniflux, and Tiny Tiny RSS, and it is commonly deployed via Docker for ease of setup. It appeals specifically to technically inclined users who want full ownership of their reading data and RSS subscriptions rather than relying on a third-party cloud reading service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Selfoss is a free, open-source, self-hosted RSS reader and content aggregation web application originally created by Tobias Zeising, with development dating back to its first commit in October 2011. It is written in PHP and designed to be lightweight and easy to deploy on almost any web host.

The project has since been maintained by community contributors, including Jan Tojnar, and is released under the GPL-3.0 license (or any later version), meaning it is free to use, modify, and self-host, including for commercial purposes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Selfoss aggregates RSS and Atom feeds alongside other content sources through an extensible plugin system, combining everything into one unified, mobile-friendly reading timeline. It supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite as database backends and allows feeds to be organized into categories or bulk-imported via OPML files.
A RESTful JSON API exposes Selfoss data to third-party clients and apps, and feed updates are triggered via a scheduled cron job or systemd timer rather than continuous background polling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Selfoss is entirely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no pricing tiers, subscriptions, or paid features. The only costs involved are for the hosting infrastructure the user chooses to run it on, such as a VPS, home server, or shared PHP hosting.

features.title: RSS and Atom feed aggregation
features.description: Subscribe to and read RSS and Atom feeds from many sources in a single unified timeline.

features.title: Extensible plugin system
features.description: An open plugin architecture lets users write custom data connectors to pull in content beyond standard feeds, such as social media sources.

features.title: Adaptive mobile-friendly layout
features.description: The reading interface automatically adapts for use on Android, iOS, and tablet devices as well as desktop browsers.

features.title: OPML import and categorization
features.description: Existing feed subscriptions can be imported via OPML files and organized into categories for easier browsing.

features.title: RESTful JSON API
features.description: A JSON API allows third-party and community-built apps to interact with a self-hosted Selfoss instance.

features.title: Lightweight PHP footprint
features.description: The application has a small installed footprint of roughly 25MB, making it easy to run on modest hosting.

features.title: Multiple database backends
features.description: Supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite, giving flexibility in how it is deployed.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL-3.0
Very lightweight and easy to self-host on modest hardware
Extensible plugin system supports sources beyond standard RSS feeds
Mobile-friendly adaptive interface works well across devices
Full data ownership since everything runs on infrastructure the user controls
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic server administration skills
Relies on a cron job or systemd timer for feed updates rather than continuous background refresh
No official company, support contracts, or SLA behind the project
Development pace depends on volunteer maintainers' available free time
No built-in hosted or cloud-managed version

faqs.question: Is Selfoss free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Selfoss is free and open-source software released under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid tiers or subscription fees.

faqs.question: Who created Selfoss?
faqs.answer: Selfoss was created by developer Tobias Zeising, with its first public commit dating to October 2011, and it is now maintained by community contributors.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Selfoss?
faqs.answer: Yes, Selfoss does not offer an official hosted cloud service; it must be installed on your own server, VPS, or web hosting.

faqs.question: What databases does Selfoss support?
faqs.answer: Selfoss supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite as database backends.

faqs.question: Can Selfoss aggregate content beyond RSS feeds?
faqs.answer: Yes, through its open plugin system, Selfoss can pull in content from social networks and other sources beyond standard RSS and Atom feeds.

faqs.question: How does Selfoss update feeds?
faqs.answer: Selfoss updates feeds via a scheduled cron job or systemd timer that triggers an HTTP endpoint or command-line update, rather than continuous background polling.

faqs.question: Does Selfoss have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Selfoss itself provides an adaptive mobile-friendly web interface; dedicated third-party mobile apps have been built by the community using its RESTful JSON API.

faqs.question: What license is Selfoss released under?
faqs.answer: Selfoss is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPL-3.0), or any later version at the user's option.

use_cases.title: Personal self-hosted news reading
use_cases.description: An individual self-hosts Selfoss on a home server or VPS to aggregate RSS feeds and other content sources into a single private reading timeline.

use_cases.title: Lightweight team content aggregation
use_cases.description: A small team deploys Selfoss on shared internal hosting to monitor industry blogs, news feeds, and announcements in one place.
use_cases.audience: Small teams and internal knowledge-sharing groups

use_cases.title: Custom content mashups via plugins
use_cases.description: A developer writes a custom plugin to pull content from a non-RSS source into Selfoss, combining it with standard feed subscriptions in one unified view.

features: Full source code access under GPL-3.0
Plugin system for custom content sources
Community support only, no official vendor support

enrichment.short_description: Sellfy is a cloud-based e-commerce platform built for creators to sell digital downloads, physical merchandise, subscriptions, and print-on-demand products from a single online store with no transaction fees.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered monthly subscription based on annual sales volume, with a discount for annual billing
enrichment.starting_price: $29 per month billed annually (Starter plan) or $39 per month billed monthly
enrichment.seo_title: Sellfy Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sellfy is an e-commerce platform for creators selling digital products, merch, and subscriptions. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sellfy is a cloud-based e-commerce platform founded in 2011 and headquartered in Riga, Latvia, built specifically for independent creators, YouTubers, musicians, designers, course creators, and small business owners who want to sell digital downloads, physical merchandise, subscription products, and print-on-demand items from one unified storefront. The company is led by founder and CEO Maris Dagis and employs roughly 32 people across its team, placing it in the small-to-mid-sized software company category. Sellfy positions itself as a simpler, lower-cost alternative to larger e-commerce platforms like Shopify for creators who primarily sell a smaller catalog of digital or creator-branded products rather than running a complex multi-category retail operation. Its core feature set includes a drag-and-drop store editor with customizable themes and the ability to add custom code, support for a custom domain, built-in email marketing tools, customer review collection, PayPal and Stripe payment processing, a free SSL certificate, mobile-optimized storefronts, and VAT and tax settings for handling international sales. On its Business and Premium tiers, Sellfy also offers custom checkout fields, product upselling, abandoned cart recovery emails, and support for an affiliate marketing program, along with the ability to remove Sellfy's own branding from the storefront. Pricing is structured around three subscription tiers gated by annual sales volume rather than by number of products or storage: the Starter plan is priced at 39 dollars per month billed monthly (or 29 dollars per month if billed annually, a 25 percent discount) and supports up to 10,000 dollars in annual sales; the Business plan is 79 dollars per month monthly (59 dollars per month annually) and supports up to 50,000 dollars in annual sales; and the Premium plan is 159 dollars per month monthly (119 dollars per month annually) and supports up to 200,000 dollars in annual sales, while also adding higher file size limits of 20GB versus 10GB on lower tiers, product and design migration assistance, and priority customer support. Notably, all Sellfy plans charge zero platform transaction fees on sales, meaning creators only pay their payment processor's standard fees (from PayPal or Stripe) rather than an additional cut to Sellfy itself, which differentiates it from some competitors that layer a percentage fee on top of subscription pricing. All plans include unlimited products across formats (digital, physical, subscription, and print-on-demand), making the sales-volume cap the primary constraint that pushes growing sellers to upgrade tiers. Sellfy competes in the creator commerce space against platforms like Gumroad, Payhip, and Shopify, and its reported annual recurring revenue has been estimated at around 3.1 million dollars with a company valuation near 9.2 million dollars based on third-party financial tracking sources.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sellfy is a cloud-based e-commerce platform founded in 2011 and headquartered in Riga, Latvia, built for independent creators and small business owners who want to sell digital downloads, physical merchandise, subscriptions, and print-on-demand products from a single online store. The company was founded by Maris Dagis, who continues to serve as CEO, and employs roughly 32 people.

Sellfy positions itself as a simpler, lower-cost alternative to full-featured e-commerce platforms for creators whose primary need is a straightforward storefront rather than a complex, multi-category retail operation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sellfy provides a drag-and-drop store editor with customizable themes, custom code support, and custom domain connection, along with built-in email marketing, customer review collection, and PayPal and Stripe payment processing. All storefronts include a free SSL certificate, mobile optimization, and VAT and tax settings for international sales.

Higher tiers add custom checkout fields, product upselling, abandoned cart recovery, and affiliate program support, plus the ability to remove Sellfy branding from the storefront and access to product and design migration assistance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sellfy's three plans are gated by annual sales volume rather than product count: Starter (up to $10k in annual sales) starts at $39 per month monthly or $29 per month billed annually; Business (up to $50k in annual sales) is $79 monthly or $59 annually; and Premium (up to $200k in annual sales) is $159 monthly or $119 annually. Annual billing offers a 25 percent discount across all tiers.

All Sellfy plans charge zero transaction fees on sales, so sellers only pay standard PayPal or Stripe processing fees rather than an additional platform cut, unlike some competing creator commerce platforms.

features.title: Drag-and-drop store builder
features.description: Customize a storefront with themes, a visual editor, and the ability to add custom code for further personalization.

features.title: Zero transaction fees
features.description: Sellfy does not charge a percentage cut on sales beyond standard PayPal and Stripe processing fees, on any plan.

features.title: Multi-format product support
features.description: Sell digital downloads, physical merchandise, subscription products, and print-on-demand items from a single storefront.

features.title: Built-in email marketing
features.description: Send marketing emails and collect customer reviews directly through the platform without a separate tool.

features.title: Custom domain and SSL
features.description: Connect a custom domain to the storefront with a free SSL certificate included on all plans.

features.title: Cart abandonment recovery
features.description: Automatically follow up with customers who leave items in their cart without completing checkout, on Business and Premium tiers.

features.title: Affiliate program support
features.description: Let affiliates promote products and earn commissions directly through the storefront on Business and Premium tiers.

features.title: Print-on-demand integration
features.description: Sell custom-branded merchandise without holding inventory through built-in print-on-demand product support.

pros: No transaction fees on any plan beyond standard payment processor charges
Simple setup aimed specifically at creators rather than general retailers
Supports digital, physical, subscription, and print-on-demand products in one store
Annual billing offers a meaningful 25% discount versus monthly pricing
Built-in email marketing and review collection reduce the need for extra tools
cons: Plans are capped by annual sales volume, forcing upgrades as a store grows
Lacks the deep customization and app ecosystem of larger platforms like Shopify
Sellfy branding remains visible on the lower Starter tier
Priority support and migration assistance are reserved for the top Premium tier
Smaller company size relative to major e-commerce competitors may mean fewer resources for rapid feature development

faqs.question: How much does Sellfy cost?
faqs.answer: Sellfy's Starter plan starts at $39 per month billed monthly ($29 per month billed annually) for up to $10,000 in annual sales, with Business and Premium tiers priced higher for larger sales volumes.

faqs.question: Does Sellfy charge transaction fees?
faqs.answer: No, Sellfy does not charge any platform transaction fees on any plan. Sellers only pay standard processing fees to PayPal or Stripe.

faqs.question: When was Sellfy founded?
faqs.answer: Sellfy was founded in 2011 and is headquartered in Riga, Latvia.

faqs.question: What products can I sell on Sellfy?
faqs.answer: Sellfy supports digital downloads, physical merchandise, subscription products, and print-on-demand items, all from a single storefront.

faqs.question: Does Sellfy have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Sellfy does not offer a permanent free plan; all tiers are paid subscriptions, though a free trial is typically available.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sellfy's plans?
faqs.answer: The three plans (Starter, Business, Premium) are differentiated primarily by the annual sales volume they support, along with features like cart recovery, affiliate support, and priority support on higher tiers.

faqs.question: Can I use my own domain with Sellfy?
faqs.answer: Yes, all Sellfy plans support connecting a custom domain along with a free SSL certificate.

faqs.question: Who founded Sellfy?
faqs.answer: Sellfy was founded by Maris Dagis, who continues to serve as the company's CEO.

use_cases.title: Digital product sales for creators
use_cases.description: A YouTuber or course creator uses Sellfy to sell digital downloads such as templates, presets, or online courses directly to their audience.
use_cases.audience: Content creators, YouTubers, and course creators

use_cases.title: Print-on-demand merchandise
use_cases.description: A designer or artist uses Sellfy's print-on-demand integration to sell custom-branded merchandise without holding physical inventory.

use_cases.title: Subscription-based content sales
use_cases.description: A musician or membership-based creator uses Sellfy's subscription product support to sell recurring access to exclusive content.

features: 20GB file size limit (vs 10GB on lower tiers)

enrichment.short_description: Semaphore (Semaphore CI) is a cloud-native continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) platform that runs code builds, tests, and deployments with per-second usage-based pricing and a free monthly credit tier.
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based pay-as-you-go pricing (billed per minute of compute) with a monthly free credit allowance and paid support add-ons; free Enterprise Edition for qualifying smaller companies
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier includes $15 of monthly usage credit; usage billed from $0.0025 per minute for self-hosted agents
enrichment.company_size: 11-50 employees (Rendered Text, the company behind Semaphore, has approximately 46 employees)
enrichment.seo_title: Semaphore CI/CD Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Semaphore (Semaphore CI) is a cloud-native CI/CD platform. See 2026 usage-based pricing, key features, pros and cons, and alternatives like GitHub Actions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Semaphore, commonly known as Semaphore CI, is a cloud-native continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) platform built by Rendered Text, a software company founded in 2009 by Marko Anastasov and Darko Fabijan and headquartered in Novi Sad, Serbia. The Semaphore product itself launched publicly in March 2012, making it one of the longer-running hosted CI platforms still actively developed today. Rendered Text, the company behind it, operates with a relatively small team of roughly 46 employees as of recent reporting, and the company has said Semaphore is used by more than 300,000 organizations globally, ranging from startups to Fortune 500 companies, with SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications supporting its enterprise credibility. Semaphore lets teams define pipelines that build, test, and deploy code automatically on every commit or pull request, running jobs on Ubuntu (x86 and ARM), macOS, or self-hosted agents, with support for parallelized test execution, Docker layer caching, and deployment automation to common cloud targets. Pricing on Semaphore's cloud (SaaS) platform is usage-based and billed per second of compute time rather than per seat: every account receives 15 dollars of free usage credit each month, equivalent to roughly 2,000 minutes on a standard 2-vCPU Ubuntu x64 machine, along with 20 concurrent jobs, 20GB of monthly egress, and 100GB of monthly storage by default. Beyond the free credit, compute is billed per minute at rates that vary by machine type for example, Ubuntu ARM machines start around 0.003 dollars per minute for a 2-vCPU instance, Ubuntu x64 machines start around 0.0075 dollars per minute, macOS machines run about 0.09 dollars per minute for a 4-vCPU instance, and self-hosted agents are billed at a flat 0.0025 dollars per minute regardless of machine type. There are no per-seat license fees and no charge for idle time, since teams only pay for compute actually consumed running pipelines. For teams that want faster or more hands-on support, Semaphore sells optional monthly add-ons layered on top of usage pricing, including Basic support at 50 dollars per month, Priority Response support at 250 dollars per month, and higher-tier SLA plans at 500 and 750 dollars per month, plus separate paid customer success and engineering-hours packages for teams that want migration help or performance tuning. Notably, Semaphore introduced a new enterprise pricing approach in 2025 that makes its self-hosted Enterprise Edition completely free for companies with under 5 million dollars in annual revenue and up to 50 users, alongside a guarantee of at least 50 percent lower CI/CD spend compared to a customer's existing setup on its hosted SaaS platform, with migration support included. Semaphore competes in the CI/CD space with GitHub Actions, CircleCI, GitLab CI/CD, and Buildkite, and it differentiates primarily on raw pipeline execution speed, per-second granular billing with no idle costs, and increasingly generous terms for smaller companies through its free Enterprise Edition offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Semaphore is a cloud-native CI/CD platform built by Rendered Text, a company founded in 2009 by Marko Anastasov and Darko Fabijan in Novi Sad, Serbia. The Semaphore product itself launched in March 2012, making it one of the earlier hosted continuous integration platforms still in active use today.

Rendered Text remains a relatively small, independent company with around 46 employees, and reports that Semaphore is used by more than 300,000 organizations worldwide, from early-stage startups to Fortune 500 enterprises, and holds SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Semaphore runs automated build, test, and deployment pipelines on every commit or pull request, supporting Ubuntu (x86 and ARM), macOS, and self-hosted agent machines. It offers parallelized test execution, Docker layer caching, and deployment automation to common cloud targets to speed up CI/CD workflows.

Pricing is billed per second of compute usage rather than per seat, so teams pay only for the pipeline time they actually consume, with concurrency, storage, and egress limits included by default on the free tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Every Semaphore account gets 15 dollars of free monthly usage credit, roughly 2,000 minutes on a standard 2-vCPU Ubuntu x64 machine, along with 20 concurrent jobs and default storage and egress allowances. Beyond that, compute is billed per minute, starting around 0.003 dollars per minute for ARM machines and 0.0025 dollars per minute for self-hosted agents, with macOS machines priced higher at around 0.09 dollars per minute.

Optional paid support tiers range from 50 dollars per month for basic email support up to 750 dollars per month for premium SLA coverage, and Semaphore's self-hosted Enterprise Edition is offered free to companies under 5 million dollars in annual revenue with up to 50 users.

features.title: Per-second usage-based billing
features.description: Compute is billed by the second, prorated per minute, with no per-seat license fees or charges for idle time.

features.title: Multi-platform build agents
features.description: Run pipelines on Ubuntu x86, Ubuntu ARM, macOS, or self-hosted agent machines depending on workload needs.

features.title: Parallelized test execution
features.description: Split and run test suites in parallel across multiple machines to reduce overall pipeline duration.

features.title: Docker layer caching
features.description: Cache Docker image layers between builds to speed up containerized pipeline runs.

features.title: Free monthly usage credit
features.description: Every account receives 15 dollars of free compute credit each month, plus default concurrency, storage, and egress allowances.

features.title: Free Enterprise Edition for smaller companies
features.description: Companies with under $5M in annual revenue and up to 50 users can run Semaphore's self-hosted Enterprise Edition at no cost.

features.title: SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certification
features.description: Semaphore holds SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications, supporting its use by security-conscious and regulated organizations.

features.title: Optional priority support tiers
features.description: Paid support add-ons ranging from basic email support to premium 24/7 SLA coverage are available on top of usage pricing.

pros: Per-second usage billing means no charges for idle compute or unused seats
Generous free monthly credit and default limits for smaller projects
Free self-hosted Enterprise Edition available for qualifying smaller companies
Long operating history since 2012 with a mature, stable platform
Strong compliance posture with SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications
cons: Usage-based pricing can be harder to predict and budget for than flat per-seat plans
macOS build minutes are notably more expensive than Linux minutes
Higher-tier support and success packages add significant cost on top of usage pricing
Smaller company size relative to larger CI/CD competitors like CircleCI or GitLab
Enterprise-grade support requires separate paid add-ons rather than being bundled

faqs.question: How much does Semaphore CI/CD cost?
faqs.answer: Semaphore's cloud platform is usage-based, billed per minute of compute, with 15 dollars of free credit included every month. Rates start around 0.0025 dollars per minute for self-hosted agents and scale up depending on machine type.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Semaphore?
faqs.answer: Yes, Semaphore includes a monthly free usage credit of 15 dollars for its cloud platform, and its self-hosted Enterprise Edition is free for companies under $5M in annual revenue with up to 50 users.

faqs.question: When was Semaphore founded?
faqs.answer: Semaphore launched in March 2012, built by Rendered Text, a company founded in 2009 by Marko Anastasov and Darko Fabijan.

faqs.question: Where is Semaphore headquartered?
faqs.answer: Semaphore's parent company, Rendered Text, is headquartered in Novi Sad, Serbia.

faqs.question: Does Semaphore support macOS builds?
faqs.answer: Yes, Semaphore supports macOS build agents, though they are priced higher per minute than Ubuntu-based machines.

faqs.question: Does Semaphore charge per seat?
faqs.answer: No, Semaphore does not charge per-seat license fees on its usage-based cloud plans; billing is based on compute minutes consumed.

faqs.question: What certifications does Semaphore hold?
faqs.answer: Semaphore is SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certified, supporting adoption by security-conscious and regulated organizations.

faqs.question: What are Semaphore's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Semaphore competes with CI/CD platforms including GitHub Actions, CircleCI, GitLab CI/CD, and Buildkite.

use_cases.title: Automated build and test pipelines
use_cases.description: A software team uses Semaphore to automatically build and run test suites on every commit and pull request, catching issues before merge.

use_cases.title: Multi-platform continuous deployment
use_cases.description: A mobile or cross-platform team uses Semaphore's Ubuntu, macOS, and ARM agents to build and deploy across multiple target platforms from one pipeline.

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious CI/CD for smaller companies
use_cases.description: A smaller company under Semaphore's revenue threshold runs the free self-hosted Enterprise Edition to get enterprise CI/CD features without licensing cost.

pricing_plans.price: $15 of free usage credit per month
features: Approximately 2,000 minutes on a 2-vCPU Ubuntu x64 machine
No per-seat fees or idle charges
Free for companies under $5M annual revenue with up to 50 users
Guaranteed 50% lower CI/CD spend vs existing setup on hosted plans

enrichment.short_description: Sematext is an observability platform providing log management, infrastructure monitoring, application performance monitoring (APM), real user monitoring, and synthetic monitoring in one SaaS product with usage-based pricing.
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based tiered pricing (per GB for logs, per host for infrastructure monitoring, per agent for APM) with a free tier and 14-day free trial
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available; paid Logs plans start around $5 per month, Infrastructure Monitoring from $2.80 per host per month
enrichment.company_size: 11-50 employees (approximately 16-18 employees as of recent reporting)
enrichment.seo_title: Sematext Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sematext is a log management and observability platform (logs, metrics, APM, monitoring). See 2026 pricing, key features, pros and cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sematext is an observability and monitoring SaaS platform founded in 2007 by Otis Gospodnetic and headquartered in Brooklyn, New York. Despite operating for nearly two decades and serving a wide customer base, the company has remained deliberately lean, reportedly running with around 16 to 18 employees as of recent reporting, a notably small team for a company competing against much larger observability vendors like Datadog, New Relic, and Elastic. Sematext's platform, marketed as Sematext Cloud, unifies several observability capabilities that are often sold as separate products elsewhere: Sematext Logs for centralized log aggregation and search, Sematext Infrastructure Monitoring for server and container metrics, Sematext Service Monitoring (its APM offering) for application performance and distributed tracing, and Sematext Synthetics for uptime and browser-based synthetic monitoring alongside real user monitoring (RUM). The company positions its pricing as being roughly 25 percent the cost of market leaders such as Datadog, built around transparent, granular metering rather than large fixed data buckets that often lead to overage surprises. Sematext Logs pricing starts with a Basic tier priced at 5 dollars per month covering roughly 500MB of daily log volume with 7-day retention, with additional data billed at approximately 0.10 dollars per GB received and 0.15 dollars per GB stored beyond the included allotment, while Standard and Pro tiers scale up data volume and retention with custom rates. Infrastructure Monitoring starts at 2.80 dollars per host per month on the Basic tier with 1-day retention, with a 10 percent discount available for annual billing, and Standard and Pro tiers extend retention further. Sematext's APM product, Service Monitoring, is priced per monitored agent starting at 8.64 dollars per agent per month on the Basic tier with 1-day retention. Synthetic monitoring is available on a pay-as-you-go basis at 2 dollars per HTTP monitor or 7 dollars per browser-based monitor per month, with a 5 dollar monthly minimum, plus Standard and Pro tiers offering larger defined monitor allowances, all with 30-day result retention. Free tiers are available for the Logs and Infrastructure Monitoring products, and every paid plan includes unlimited users and unlimited data sources with no fixed per-seat charges, differentiating Sematext from vendors that charge per host or per user on top of data volume. All Sematext plans include a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, and the company emphasizes month-to-month billing flexibility with no long-term contracts required, along with more than 100 pre-built integrations and service-specific dashboards for common infrastructure and application stacks. Sematext is a practical option for engineering teams, DevOps groups, and smaller companies that want unified logs, metrics, APM, and uptime monitoring without paying enterprise-tier observability vendor pricing, though its much smaller company size compared to competitors may mean a narrower support organization and slower pace of major platform overhauls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sematext is an observability and monitoring platform founded in 2007 by Otis Gospodnetic and headquartered in Brooklyn, New York. The company operates with a notably lean team of roughly 16 to 18 employees despite competing in the same market as much larger vendors like Datadog and New Relic.

Its flagship product, Sematext Cloud, unifies log management, infrastructure monitoring, application performance monitoring (APM), real user monitoring, and synthetic monitoring into a single platform rather than requiring separate tools for each.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sematext Logs centralizes log aggregation and search across an infrastructure, while Sematext Infrastructure Monitoring tracks server and container-level metrics. Sematext Service Monitoring provides APM and distributed tracing for applications, and Sematext Synthetics offers uptime checks, browser-based synthetic monitoring, and real user monitoring.

The platform includes more than 100 pre-built integrations with service-specific dashboards for common infrastructure and application technology stacks, along with transparent, granular usage-based metering instead of large fixed data buckets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sematext Logs starts at 5 dollars per month on the Basic tier for around 500MB of daily volume with 7-day retention, with additional usage billed per GB received and stored. Infrastructure Monitoring starts at 2.80 dollars per host per month with 1-day retention, and Service Monitoring (APM) starts at 8.64 dollars per agent per month, both with higher Standard and Pro tiers for extended retention and volume.

Synthetic monitoring is billed pay-as-you-go at 2 dollars per HTTP monitor or 7 dollars per browser monitor monthly, with a 5 dollar minimum. Free tiers exist for Logs and Infrastructure Monitoring, and all plans include a 14-day free trial with no credit card required and no long-term contract commitment.

features.title: Unified log management
features.description: Centralized log aggregation, search, and analysis across servers, containers, and applications in Sematext Logs.

features.title: Infrastructure monitoring
features.description: Server and container-level metrics monitoring with automatic charts and reports in Sematext Infrastructure.

features.title: Application performance monitoring (APM)
features.description: Service-level performance metrics and distributed tracing through Sematext Service Monitoring, priced per agent.

features.title: Synthetic and real user monitoring
features.description: Uptime checks, browser-based synthetic monitoring, and real user monitoring for tracking site and application performance from the user's perspective.

features.title: Transparent usage-based pricing
features.description: Granular per-GB, per-host, and per-agent pricing with no large fixed data buckets or hidden overage charges.

features.title: 100+ pre-built integrations
features.description: Ready-made integrations and service-specific dashboards for common infrastructure and application technology stacks.

features.title: Unlimited users and sources
features.description: Every plan includes unlimited users and monitored data sources with no additional per-seat charges.

features.title: No long-term contracts
features.description: Month-to-month billing flexibility with no required long-term commitment on any plan.

pros: Roughly 25% the cost of larger observability vendors like Datadog according to the company's own positioning
Unifies logs, infrastructure monitoring, APM, and synthetics in a single platform
Transparent per-unit pricing avoids large fixed data buckets and surprise overages
Free tiers available for Logs and Infrastructure Monitoring products
No long-term contract requirement and flexible month-to-month billing
cons: Notably small team (roughly 16-18 employees) relative to major competitors, which may limit support capacity
Usage-based pricing across multiple products (logs, hosts, agents, monitors) can be complex to estimate upfront
Retention periods on Basic tiers are short (1 to 7 days) before requiring an upgrade
Less brand recognition and market share compared to Datadog, New Relic, or Elastic
Custom pricing on Standard and Pro tiers requires additional research or a sales conversation to estimate true cost at scale

faqs.question: How much does Sematext cost?
faqs.answer: Sematext uses usage-based pricing across its products. Logs starts at $5/month, Infrastructure Monitoring starts at $2.80 per host per month, and Service Monitoring (APM) starts at $8.64 per agent per month, each on Basic tiers with limited retention.

faqs.question: Does Sematext offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, free tiers are available for Sematext Logs and Sematext Infrastructure Monitoring, and all paid plans include a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: When was Sematext founded?
faqs.answer: Sematext was founded in 2007 by Otis Gospodnetic.

faqs.question: Where is Sematext headquartered?
faqs.answer: Sematext is headquartered in Brooklyn, New York, United States.

faqs.question: What does Sematext monitor?
faqs.answer: Sematext provides log management, infrastructure monitoring, application performance monitoring (APM), real user monitoring, and synthetic (uptime) monitoring.

faqs.question: Is Sematext cheaper than Datadog?
faqs.answer: Sematext positions its pricing at roughly 25% the cost of market leaders like Datadog, based on the company's own comparisons, using transparent per-unit metering rather than fixed data buckets.

faqs.question: Does Sematext require a long-term contract?
faqs.answer: No, Sematext offers month-to-month billing flexibility with no long-term contract requirement on its plans.

faqs.question: How is Sematext's APM priced?
faqs.answer: Sematext's Service Monitoring (APM) product is priced per monitored agent, starting at $8.64 per agent per month on the Basic tier.

use_cases.title: Centralized log management for DevOps
use_cases.description: A DevOps team uses Sematext Logs to aggregate and search logs from servers, containers, and applications in one centralized dashboard.

use_cases.title: Application performance monitoring
use_cases.description: A backend engineering team uses Sematext Service Monitoring to track application performance metrics and trace slow requests across microservices.

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious full-stack observability
use_cases.description: A smaller company adopts Sematext Cloud to get unified logs, infrastructure monitoring, and synthetic checks at a lower cost than enterprise observability vendors.
use_cases.audience: Small to mid-sized engineering organizations with budget constraints
